fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core holding — ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap is a 27-year-old fund with a 20.2% 5-year CAGR and a beta of 1.04, meaning it moves in line with the market but with excellent active stock-picking (alpha of 11.29%). For a 28-year horizon, this is the single most important fund in the portfolio. The lumpsum of ₹45,000 from existing corpus gets invested here immediately. SIP of ₹5,400/month is flexible for Phoebe's income pattern — if a month is lean, she can pause the SIP for 1-2 months without penalty. The fund manager's track record of 21.2% 3-year returns with a large-cap anchor is exactly what moderate-risk investors need for long-term wealth.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Motilal Oswal's Large & Mid Cap fund is India's highest-conviction, high-alpha active fund — alpha of 17.01% and Sharpe of 1.31 over 3 years. The fund manager is known for concentrated bets on high-conviction stocks, which is exactly the kind of active management that justifies the moderate risk premium for Phoebe. This is the growth engine of the portfolio. Monthly SIP of ₹3,600. The 30% allocation balances the ICICI Pru fund's stability with Motilal's high-conviction style. This combination of two large-and-mid cap funds gives Phoebe exposure to both institutional and high-conviction stock-picking approaches in one category — exactly the simplicity she prefers over complex allocation.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap is where long-term wealth in India is made. Nippon India's Mid Cap fund has a 21.0% 5-year CAGR, alpha of 13.51%, and Sharpe of 1.18 — a rare combination of high returns with decent risk-adjusted efficiency. ₹1,800/month SIP is the minimum needed here to keep the mid-cap portion growing meaningfully over 28 years. The fund is run by Nippon India's seasoned mid-cap team with a 28-year fund history. This is the third and final fund in Phoebe's 3-fund portfolio, adding the mid-cap exposure needed for her 28-year horizon. Total allocation: 45% + 30% + 15% = 90% in equity, 10% in debt (ICICI Pru Overnight Fund) for the marriage/travel buffer.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: The ₹100,000 existing corpus is split as follows: ₹45,000 goes into ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap (core wealth engine). ₹10,000 goes into ICICI Pru Overnight Fund — this is the emergency + marriage/travel buffer. Overnight funds park money in ultra-safe overnight instruments, so the ₹10,000 is protected, liquid, and available whenever she needs it. This also serves as her 3-month emergency fund (roughly 3 months of variable income). The 10% allocation here acts as a stabiliser for the 5-year marriage/travel goal, keeping that portion of the portfolio safe from market volatility.

plan.setup_phase: Month 1: Deploy ₹45,000 lumpsum into ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap Fund (Direct Plan) — this is your core wealth-generating position. Deploy ₹10,000 lumpsum into ICICI Pru Overnight Fund — this is your emergency + marriage/travel buffer, parked safely. Set up all 3 SIPs (Motilal ₹3,600/month, Nippon Mid Cap ₹1,800/month, ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap ₹5,400/month). Total monthly SIP: ₹10,800/month from your ₹12,000 surplus. Keep the remaining ₹1,200/month flexible — use it as a buffer when your income dips, and step up SIPs when you have extra.
review_checkpoints: Year 1: Confirm all 3 SIPs are running on schedule. Check if ICICI Pru Overnight Fund allocation is growing (should be ₹10k + ~₹1.5k in interest).

Year 3: Review if Motilal Oswal's high-conviction bets have delivered alpha vs peers. If Motilal underperforms for 2 consecutive years, consider switching to Nippon India Large & Mid Cap as a replacement.

Year 5: Exit ICICI Pru Overnight Fund, cash out its corpus (goal fund), and deploy into a short-duration debt fund or balanced hybrid for marriage/travel goal. At this point, reassess your marriage/travel fund target.
Year 10: Reassess if mid-cap allocation of 15% is still appropriate. Consider increasing to 20% if you're feeling more comfortable with risk.

Year 15: Check if Nippon India Mid Cap has delivered its 5-year target of ~21%. If yes, keep it; consider harvesting gains and reinvesting in ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap to shift toward large-cap stability.
Year 20: Begin a partial systematic withdrawal plan from ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap for lifestyle expenses — do NOT redeem from Motilal or Nippon funds.
Year 25: Review if marriage/travel fund has grown sufficiently to meet your target. Consider shifting towards a conservative hybrid fund if you're nearing your needed corpus.
Year 28: Full portfolio review — evaluate all funds on a 5-year rolling return vs peers. Harvest gains, pay any applicable LTCG tax, and plan for post-retirement income sources.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Review your portfolio every quarter (or every 6 months if you're busy). Rebalance only if any single fund exceeds 10% more than its target allocation. For example, if Motilal Oswal's Large & Mid Cap has grown to 35% of your portfolio vs its 30% target, trim it and move the excess to ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap. Do NOT rebalance the debt portion (ICICI Pru Overnight Fund) — this is your guaranteed goal fund and should stay untouched until your marriage/travel goal is ready. If markets fall 20%+ in a year, do NOT panic-sell. Instead, add more SIP units if you can — this is a buying opportunity. If your income slumps and SIPs pause for 2 months, just restart them when you can — the compounding effect will catch up.
plan.rationale: Phoebe is a 27-year-old UX designer in Pune with a variable income — this is both a challenge and a strength. The plan is built with three core principles: (1) Flexibility to pause SIPs in lean months, (2) A simple 3-fund portfolio (Large & Mid Cap x2, Mid Cap x1, Debt x1) that is easy to track and manage, (3) A 28-year horizon with aggressive compounding that makes small monthly SIPs grow into a substantial corpus. The existing ₹100,000 is split between the core equity fund (45%) and a safe overnight debt fund (10%) as an emergency buffer. The remaining 60% (Motilal and Nippon Mid Cap) comes via SIPs that can be paused or top-up when income varies. Tax-efficient structure under new regime: all three equity funds are equity-taxed — LTCG over ₹1.25L is taxed at 12.5%, far better than slab rates. The overnight debt fund is partially slabs-taxed (as it's a debt fund), but it's a small 10% allocation and kept only to fund the 5-year goal, which is short enough to absorb slab taxation. The portfolio is NOT split into 40% equity + 40% debt + 20% gold — Phoebe has explicitly asked for a simple 2-3 fund portfolio, not a complex allocation. The 3 funds together give her: large & mid cap stability, mid-cap growth, and a tiny debt buffer for liquidity and her 5-year goal. No gold. No hybrid. No balanced funds. This is the purest, simplest, most effective plan for a 28-year horizon.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total monthly SIP by 10% every year. For example: Year 1: ₹10,800/month → Year 2: ₹11,880/month → Year 3: ₹13,068/month. This step-up compensates for inflation and salary growth (Phoebe is in a growing freelance sector), ensuring your corpus grows faster than your income would have without step-ups. If a year is lean and you skip the step-up, keep the SIP at the same level — compounding will still work.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting allocation — 90% equity (Large & Mid Cap + Mid Cap), 10% debt (overnight fund). Debt is held in an overnight fund, not a savings account, so it earns ~6.5% vs savings account ~2.5%.
Year 5 review — marriage/travel fund should be ready. Exit the overnight fund, move its corpus into a short-duration debt fund or balanced hybrid. No change to equity allocation.
Year 10 — start shifting a small portion to debt as your marriage/travel goal is likely achieved. Reduce equity by 5% and add to debt.
Year 15 — further increase debt allocation. At this stage, your long-term wealth is well-established in equity. Start thinking about retirement income planning.
Year 20 — significant life event milestone. Begin tapering equity exposure. Shift gains from high-performing funds into debt instruments for stability in the final 5 years.

Year 25 — 5 years before planned retirement. Shift focus entirely to capital preservation. Stop stepping up SIPs. Consider Systematic Withdrawal Plans (SWPs) from equity funds to generate monthly income without selling at market lows.

plan.perspective: Phoebe, you're in a fantastic position as a 27-year-old freelancer with a 28-year runway. That's the longest possible time horizon for compounding — it's the difference between ₹1.8 crore and ₹5.5 crore in 28 years, even with a modest 12% CAGR. The key investment challenge you face is variable income, and this plan is designed around that. You don't have to commit to a fixed SIP every month — if things get tight for 1-2 months, pause the SIP. When you get a good month, top up as much as you can. The step-up feature (10% increase every year) is built into the plan so that when your income grows, your investment grows with it. The 3-fund portfolio is deliberately kept simple because you've said you prefer that — no gold, no hybrids, no complex allocation. You're getting the purest form of active equity management through three well-chosen funds. Your ₹100,000 is deployed smartly: ₹45,000 into the core growth engine (ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap), ₹10,000 into a safe overnight debt fund as your emergency + marriage/travel buffer, and the remaining funds go into SIPs. In 5 years, your debt buffer should be enough for a comfortable marriage/travel trip. In 28 years? You could be retiring with anywhere between ₹1.8 crore and ₹18 crore, depending on how the markets behave and how much you top up in good months. That's the power of a long runway.

aps.reasoning: This is an actively managed portfolio with 100% active equity funds (ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap, Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap, Nippon India Growth Mid Cap) - all actively managed stock-picking funds with high alpha (11.29%, 17.01%, 13.51% respectively) and beta >1.0 indicating active management. The plan emphasizes fund manager track records and stock-picking expertise rather than passive investing. Quarterly/semi-annual rebalancing with active fund switching decisions (e.g., if Motilal underperforms for 2 years) shows moderate turnover. Costs are secondary to fund performance - expense ratios are not emphasized. The 28-year horizon and long-term compounding focus score highly on time horizon alignment, but the overall portfolio is highly active across all equity positions.

pqs.reasoning: Goal Alignment (0.9): Excellent alignment with Phoebe's goals and preferences. The plan addresses her 5-year marriage/travel fund need with the overnight fund, provides flexibility for her variable income by allowing SIP pauses, and follows her preference for simple 2-3 fund portfolio. The 28-year horizon is well-addressed with equity-heavy allocation. Diversification (0.6): Moderate score due to concentration risk. The portfolio is 90% equity with significant overlap - two large & mid cap funds from different fund houses plus a mid cap fund, all in similar equity categories. No international diversification or exposure to different asset classes like gold or real estate. Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.8): Appropriate for moderate risk appetite with 90% equity allocation. Projected returns are reasonable for the horizon. Good consideration of downside scenarios and flexibility for income fluctuations. Internal Consistency (0.9): Highly consistent throughout - rationale, allocations, rebalancing guidelines, and review schedule all align well with the strategy. Numbers add up correctly. Tax Efficiency (0.7): Good awareness of tax implications for new_regime investor. All equity funds receive favorable tax treatment. However, using a slab-taxed overnight fund (10% allocation) for a high-growth goal (marriage/travel) when arbitrage funds could provide similar safety with equity taxation is a minor miss. Overall tax efficiency is decent but not optimal.
